### AF 2024-05-29 1337

### AF 2024-05-29 1337
(00:08:13) Prosecutors charged Donald Trump with 34 felony counts of falsifying business records, elevating the charges from a misdemeanor by alleging the falsification was done to further a secondary, unspecified crime.

### AF 2024-05-29 1337
(00:09:24) The prosecution in the Manhattan case argued that Donald Trump falsified records to manipulate an election, constituting a secondary crime that transformed the misdemeanor into a felony.

### AF 2024-05-29 1337

### AF 2024-05-29 1337

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(00:30:17) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] stated that election night 2016 was the greatest day of his life and that Donald Trump had been a part of his life for the past five years.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(00:31:52) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that Donald Trump was a real leader who cared about [[United States|Americans]] and was not part of a "pedophile, satanic elite."

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(00:32:44) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] criticized the media for asking the new [[Joe Biden]] press secretary about whether the color scheme of [[Air Force One]] would be changed from the Trump administration.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(00:37:03) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] condemned [[Mike Pence]] for attending the [[Joe Biden]] inauguration, calling it a betrayal after serving on the same ticket as Donald Trump and experiencing investigations and impeachment alongside him.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(00:41:25) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that [[Joe Biden]]'s call for unity was contradicted by his immediate reversal of Trump policies and his administration's characterization of Trump supporters as terrorists and white supremacists.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(00:41:37) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] noted that Donald Trump left office with 74 million votes and a 95% approval rating within the [[Republican Party]], arguing [[Joe Biden]] lacked a mandate for his sweeping actions.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(00:44:35) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that the actions of 74 million voters for Donald Trump and the [[January 6th Attack|January 6th]] rally were expressions of grievance from people who did not feel part of the system, which the [[Joe Biden]] administration would silence.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(00:46:11) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] declared [[Joe Biden]] an illegitimate president and stated "not my president," arguing that while Trump won legitimately despite voter fraud, [[Joe Biden|Biden]] won only because of rigging and fraud.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(01:00:08) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] expressed that he would miss Donald Trump's charismatic personality and "Trumpisms," contrasting it with the "cringe" politics of the [[Joe Biden]] era.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(01:06:08) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] stated that in an ideal hierarchical civilization, people would slavishly praise the [[king]] and God, rather than contemporary celebrities, and expressed a desire for an [[United States|American]] monarchy with Donald Trump as [[king]].

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(01:07:34) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that Trump's 2016 victory showed people's desire for a "[[king]]-like" leader who protects the kingdom's people, reflecting a deep longing for monarchy over liberal democracy.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(01:12:29) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] listed [[Patrick Buchanan|Pat Buchanan]], [[Lou Dobbs]], and [[Ron Paul]] as figures who discussed [[United States|America]] First themes like nationalism and opposition to globalization prior to Trump.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(01:15:34) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that many potential [[United States|America]] First supporters voted for [[Barack Obama]] in 2008 due to his populist promises on ending wars and bank bailouts, and only turned to Trump after [[Obama]]'s perceived failure.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(01:26:56) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] reminisced about the promise and excitement of Donald Trump's 2016 campaign and inauguration, contrasting it with the pain of the current [[Joe Biden]] era.

### AF 2021-01-19 0745

### AF 2021-01-19 0745

### AF 2021-01-19 0745

(00:06:56) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] recounted that he and [[Ali Alexander]] had expected the [[January 6th Attack|January 6th]] Trump rally to be a low-key conclusion to the "Stop the Steal" protests, not the major event it became.

### AF 2021-01-19 0745
(00:10:08) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] characterized Trump and [[Mike Lindell]] as "class traitor" billionaires facing suppression from the establishment.

### AF 2021-01-19 0745
(00:13:56) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] stated that January 19, 2021, was the final night of the Trump administration, with [[Joe Biden]] and [[Kamala Harris]] set to be inaugurated for the next four years.

### AF 2021-01-19 0745
(00:17:10) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] characterized the Trump administration as having "come in like a lion and out like a lamb," a theme of disappointment for the show.

### AF 2021-01-19 0745
(00:17:25) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] contrasted Trump's 2016 [[Republican Party|RNC]] nomination speech, which was full of hope and applause, with his farewell address, stating "this is not the same guy."

### AF 2021-01-19 0745
(00:18:50) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that the Trump administration's final pardons and farewell address represented a "final humiliation, betrayal, and disappointment."

### AF 2021-01-19 0745
(00:21:13) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] stated that 74 million people voted for Trump in 2020 and many still believe in the promise of Trumpism, which must be used as a practical tool to sustain the movement.

### AF 2021-01-19 0745
(00:22:22) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that focusing too much on the failures of the Trump administration would be the "final nail in the coffin" for any resistance, and that the movement must remember the hopeful "mythology" of the 2016 campaign.

### AF 2021-01-19 0745
(00:23:30) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] reported that Trump was planning to unveil over a hundred pardons and sentence commutations on his final full day, a unilateral power he had used less frequently than his predecessors.

(00:24:21) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] stated that despite having nothing left to lose, Trump's final pardons did not include loyalists like [[Julian Assange]], [[Edward Snowden]], [[Steven Crowder|Steve Bannon]], or [[United States Capitol|Capitol]] rioters, but instead rewarded enemies.

### AF 2021-01-19 0745
(00:28:08) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] characterized the Trump administration as one that "rewards its worst enemies and saboteurs and punishes its loyalists," with [[Steven Crowder|Steve Bannon]] facing jail while critics received pardons.

(00:33:30) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] criticized Trump for attacking the [[United States Capitol|Capitol]] rioters as "law breakers" and instructing the [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]] and [[U.S. Department of Justice|DOJ]] to persecute them, instead of supporting or pardoning them.

(00:35:34) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that Trump began to recapture the populist, nationalist spirit of his 2016 campaign in 2020 with executive orders on immigration and big tech, but that this momentum disappeared after the election.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:09:00) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] noted that the setups for the separate Trump and [[Joe Biden]] town halls in [[State of Florida|Florida]] and [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]] were socially distanced, making the cancellation of an in-[[person]] debate seem arbitrary.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:11:03) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] characterized [[Savannah Guthrie]]'s interview with Donald Trump as brutal and lacking impartiality, focusing on scandals like the [[White House]] event being a "super spreader" and accusations of white supremacy.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:15:16) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that Donald Trump lost a crucial opportunity to reach a large audience by not having a debate, which he needed as early voting numbers were concerning.

(00:34:25) In May, Donald Trump signed an executive order targeting tech companies like [[Facebook]] and [[Google]] concerning Section 230, instructing the [[Commerce Department]] to draft a petition for the [[Federal Communications Commission|FCC]].

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:45:44) The [[Cato Institute]] report stated that legal immigration almost wholly stopped in April and May 2020 after the [[U.S. Department of State|State Department]] closed consulates and Donald Trump issued a proclamation suspending new visa issuances for most immigrant categories.

(00:55:11) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] listed Donald Trump's accomplishments as including a 97% reduction in immigration, the [[Federal Communications Commission|FCC]] moving against tech censorship, 450 miles of border wall construction, and troop reductions in [[Iraq]] and [[Afghanistan]].

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:56:27) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] credited these accomplishments to Donald Trump overcoming opposition from [[Democratic Party|Democrats]], the media, intelligence agencies, and subversion from within his own party from figures like [[Paul Ryan]] and [[Mitch McConnell]].

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:00:00) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] began his monologue by reacting to a clip of Donald Trump being interviewed by [[Savannah Guthrie]] of [[NBC News|NBC]] about his recent COVID-19 diagnosis and treatment at [[Walter Reed National Military Medical Center|Walter Reed]] National Military Medical Center.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:04:24) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] criticized [[Savannah Guthrie]] for focusing on Donald Trump's COVID-19 test timing and mask-wearing habits 20 days before a presidential election.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:06:04) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] noted that [[Chris Christie]], [[who]] helped Donald Trump with debate preparation, was hospitalized with COVID-19 and publicly stated he was wrong not to wear a mask.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:07:02) [[Savannah Guthrie]] asked Donald Trump if he believed a grieving military family gave him COVID-19 during a Gold Star family event.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:07:58) Donald Trump stated that the COVID-19 disease was sent to the [[United States]] by [[China]] and should never have been allowed to happen.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:09:42) [[Savannah Guthrie]] asked Donald Trump if he supported a herd immunity strategy for COVID-19, which she described as letting young people get sick while protecting the elderly.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:10:18) Donald Trump asserted that COVID-19 happened because of [[China]] and that the [[United States]] was expected to lose 2.2 million people but had lost 210,000.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:11:02) Donald Trump criticized [[Gretchen Whitmer]], the Governor of [[State of Michigan|Michigan]], for lockdown policies while her husband could "go boating and do whatever he wants."

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:11:20) Donald Trump stated that [[New York]] lost almost 40,000 people to COVID-19 and that people were leaving the state by the thousands.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:12:15) Donald Trump denounced white supremacy during the [[NBC News|NBC]] town hall, a point [[Savannah Guthrie]] repeatedly returned to.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:13:02) [[Savannah Guthrie]] asked Donald Trump to disavow [[QAnon]], which she described as a conspiracy theory alleging [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] are a satanic pedophile ring and that Trump is its savior.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:13:35) Donald Trump stated he knew nothing about [[QAnon]] but knew they were very much against pedophilia, a point he reiterated.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:15:10) [[Savannah Guthrie]] questioned Donald Trump about retweeting a conspiracy theory to his followers alleging [[Joe Biden]] orchestrated the death of [[U.S. Department of Defense|Navy]] SEAL Team 6 to cover up the fake death of [[Osama bin Laden]].

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:16:16) [[Savannah Guthrie]] questioned Donald Trump about his repeated statements that the only way he could lose the election was if it was rigged, citing a lack of evidence for widespread fraud from his own [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]] director.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:17:17) Donald Trump stated that his campaign was spied on and that they tried to take down a duly elected president, but he would accept a peaceful transfer of power if the election was honest.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:19:15) An audience member, Jacqueline Lugo, asked Donald Trump why he only implemented a travel ban from [[China]] and not other measures after telling [[Bob Woodward]] in February that COVID-19 was airborne and deadlier than the flu.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:19:46) Donald Trump responded that [[Joe Biden]] called him xenophobic and racist for the [[China]] travel ban, which [[Anthony Fauci]] later said saved thousands of lives, and that he acted against the advice of [[Nancy Pelosi]].

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:21:37) A frontline ER doctor in the audience asked Donald Trump how he would get the [[United States]] back on track economically from the pandemic, citing hospital financial hardships and frontline worker furloughs.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:22:22) Donald Trump pointed to a record 11.4 million jobs created, predicted a phenomenal third-quarter GDP, and stated the [[United States]] had the greatest economy in its history before the pandemic.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:24:18) The doctor's daughter, Isabella, asked Donald Trump if contracting COVID-19 changed his opinion on the importance of mask-wearing, citing the toll on her healthcare worker parents.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:25:19) [[Savannah Guthrie]] cited a [[University of Washington]] model stating [[universal]] mask-wearing could cut expected COVID-19 deaths in half, which Donald Trump countered by referencing [[Anthony Fauci]]'s initial advice against masks and a recent report on mask wearers catching the virus.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:27:55) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] asserted that the [[Joe Biden]] town hall on [[ABC News|ABC]] would have perfect audio and less tough questions compared to the "cage match" interrogation of Donald Trump by [[Savannah Guthrie]].

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:28:11) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] summarized the [[NBC News|NBC]] town hall's questioning sequence: first on Donald Trump catching COVID-19, then disavowing white supremacy, then disavowing [[QAnon]], then retweeting conspiracy theories, then committing to a peaceful transfer of power, then an audience question citing [[Bob Woodward]].

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:30:14) An audience member, Christy Alonso, asked Donald Trump about his plan to make healthcare costs affordable, noting he had originally promised to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:30:37) Donald Trump stated his administration got rid of the individual mandate, always protected people with pre-existing conditions, brought costs down, and aimed to terminate the Affordable Care Act and replace it with something cheaper and better.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:32:15) [[Savannah Guthrie]] pressed Donald Trump on why, with both houses of [[Congress]] in [[Republican Party|Republican]] hands, he had repealed but not replaced the Affordable Care Act.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:33:31) An audience member, Joe White, asked Donald Trump why he didn't use his office to make a second stimulus payment a separate, targeted emergency relief package, given broad agreement on its necessity.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:33:36) Donald Trump placed blame for the stalled stimulus on [[Nancy Pelosi]], stating she "couldn't care less about the worker" and was blocking approval for political reasons, while he and [[Republican Party|Republicans]] were ready to sign.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:34:33) [[Savannah Guthrie]] questioned Donald Trump on his changing positions on stimulus negotiations, from tweeting "no more negotiations" to wanting a "big, beautiful stimulus," asking why he himself had changed positions.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:35:48) An audience member, Becky Lightman, asked Donald Trump why he wanted to keep corporate tax rates lower, as many [[United States|Americans]] believe corporations don't pay their share.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:36:19) Donald Trump argued that lower corporate tax rates caused companies to pour into the [[United States]], creating a record 160 million jobs, and that [[Joe Biden]]'s plan to raise taxes would cause a depression.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:37:21) [[Savannah Guthrie]] questioned Donald Trump about [[The New York Times]] report on his tax returns, specifically on $421 million in personally guaranteed debt coming due and [[who]] he owed the money to.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:38:59) Donald Trump stated he did not owe money to [[Russia]] and that the debt was a very small percentage of his assets' worth, calling himself "extremely under-levered."

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:41:06) [[Savannah Guthrie]] asked Donald Trump if it was true that he paid only $750 in taxes the year he was elected, to which he responded it was a "statutory number" or a "filing number."

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:41:40) Donald Trump stated that [[Hillary Clinton]] and the [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] were dealing with [[Russia]], not him, calling the [[Russia]] investigation a hoax.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:42:37) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] critiqued Donald Trump's answers on healthcare and taxes, arguing they lacked cogent detail and that discussing terms like "under-levered" or "filing number" was ineffective with a general public unfamiliar with finance.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:43:28) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that the political establishment wants managerial technocrats like [[Joe Biden]] or [[Emmanuel Macron]] [[who]] use boring, meaningless language to maintain stability, unlike Donald Trump's bold, emotive, and denotative style.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:45:57) An audience member, [[Adam Kinzinger|Adam]] Shooker, asked Donald Trump about the hypocrisy of [[Senate Republicans]] fast-tracking his [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] nominee, [[Amy Coney Barrett]], after saying it was inappropriate to push through a nominee in an election year in 2016.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:46:27) Donald Trump defended the nomination, stating the president is elected for four years, a vacancy came up, and he picked an outstanding [[person]], adding that the [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] would do the same.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:48:31) [[Savannah Guthrie]] asked Donald Trump if he expected [[Amy Coney Barrett]] to rule for him in potential election challenges, given he had mentioned wanting her confirmed for that reason.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:48:58) Donald Trump stated it would be totally up to [[Amy Coney Barrett]] and he never spoke to her about how she would rule on election cases or other specific questions, believing it would be inappropriate.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:50:09) An audience member, Mariah Green, a pro-life millennial, asked Donald Trump what protections would be in place for mothers in high-risk pregnancies if [[Roe v. Wade]] were overturned.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:50:55) Donald Trump stated he never spoke to [[Amy Coney Barrett]], [[Neil Gorsuch]], or [[Brett Kavanaugh]] about [[Roe v. Wade]], and that the issue might be sent down to the states or nothing might happen, refusing to state his personal preference to avoid influencing her.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:52:29) An audience member, Cindy Velez, asked Donald Trump what he would do to prepare law enforcement to collaborate with communities and protect innocent [[Black]] and Latino lives from police brutality.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:53:18) Donald Trump cited a police reform bill by Senator [[Tim Scott]] that [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] wouldn't approve and stated he had done more for the African [[United States|American]] community than any president except [[Abraham Lincoln]], citing criminal justice reform and funding for historically [[black]] colleges.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:55:11) An audience member, Paulette Dale, asked Donald Trump if he expected to pursue efforts to cut the DACA program in a second term, citing her background as a daughter of immigrants [[who]] fled religious persecution in [[Europe|Eastern Europe]].

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:55:54) Donald Trump stated they were going to take care of DACA and Dreamers, were negotiating immigration law, had built over 400 miles of border wall, and wanted people to come in legally.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702

### AF 2020-10-09 0699
(00:10:38) Donald Trump swiftly rejected the virtual debate plan, setting off a series of events that put the future of all general election debates into question.

(00:11:33) The [[Joe Biden|Biden]] campaign rejected the Trump campaign's proposal to reschedule the debates.

### AF 2020-08-27 0670
(00:28:46) Donald Trump delivered the keynote speech on the final night of the [[Republican Party|Republican National Convention]], accepting the party's nomination for [[Joe Biden|President of the United States]].

### AF 2020-08-27 0670
(00:31:56) Donald Trump imposed a travel ban on [[China]] early in the coronavirus pandemic, a policy his opponent [[Joe Biden]] criticized as xenophobic.

### AF 2020-08-27 0670
(00:36:48) Donald Trump withdrew the [[United States]] from the [[Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action|Iran nuclear deal]] negotiated by the [[Barack Obama administration|Obama administration]].

### AF 2020-08-27 0670
(00:36:58) Donald Trump recognized [[City of Jerusalem|Jerusalem]] as the capital of [[Israel]] and moved the [[United States]] [[United States Embassy in Jerusalem|embassy]] there from [[Rishon LeZion|Tel Aviv]].

### AF 2020-08-27 0670
(00:37:57) Donald Trump's administration brokered a peace agreement between [[Israel]] and the [[United Arab Emirates]], the first such deal in the [[Middle East]] in 25 years.

### AF 2020-08-27 0670

(00:38:49) Donald Trump ordered the drone strike that eliminated [[Iran|Iranian]] General [[Qasem Soleimani]], whom he described as the world's number one terrorist.

### AF 2020-08-27 0670
(00:39:04) Donald Trump kept [[United States|America]] out of new wars and brought troops home from conflicts in the [[Middle East]].

### AF 2020-08-27 0670
(00:39:50) Donald Trump oversaw a $2.5 trillion rebuilding of the [[U.S. Department of Defense|U.S. military]], which included three pay raises for service members.

### AF 2020-08-27 0670
(00:39:50) Donald Trump established the [[Space Force]] as a new branch of the [[U.S. Department of Defense|U.S. military]].

### AF 2020-08-27 0670

(00:40:29) [[Joe Biden]] supported [[China]]'s entry into the [[World Trade Organization]], a move Donald Trump characterized as an economic disaster.

(00:41:03) [[Joe Biden]] would, according to Donald Trump, return the [[United States]] to a weak foreign policy and allow [[China]] to own the [[country]].

(00:41:44) Donald Trump invoked the [[Defense Production Act]] to mobilize the production of ventilators during the coronavirus pandemic.

### AF 2020-08-27 0670
(00:43:24) Donald Trump's [[Paycheck Protection Program]] saved or supported over 50 million [[United States|American]] jobs during the pandemic-induced economic crisis.

(00:48:35) Donald Trump claimed to have done more for the African [[United States|American]] community than any president since [[Abraham Lincoln]].

### AF 2020-08-21 0667

(00:10:10) The Trump administration built the most recent 100 miles of border wall in one month, following years of legal and congressional obstacles.

(00:11:20) Donald Trump renewed his 2016 campaign vow to make [[Mexico]] pay for the border wall, suggesting a tax on remittances, which totaled $38.6 billion sent from the [[United States]] in 2019.

(00:14:00) The Trump administration had already allocated funds for an additional 430 miles of border wall, with 300 miles already under construction, bringing the total to over 700 miles either constructed, funded, or in progress.

(00:34:50) The [[Joe Biden|Biden]] campaign, factoring in super PACs, was on track to outraise and outspend the Trump campaign in the closing months of the election.

### AF 2020-03-19 0568
(00:01:40) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] announced the main story for the show would be about the coronavirus pandemic and related economic stimulus proposals. (00:01:40) [[Senate]] [[Republican Party|Republicans]] unveiled a draft proposal to provide $1,200 cash payments to individuals earning under $75,000 and $2,400 to couples earning under $150,000, with amounts tapering for higher incomes and reduced payments for those with low taxable income. (00:03:06) The [[United States Senate]] passed the first phase of a coronavirus relief package, which included free coronavirus testing and paid sick leave. (00:04:18) [[Italian Republic|Italy]] surpassed [[China]] in official confirmed deaths from coronavirus, with [[Italian Republic|Italy]] reporting 41,000 cases compared to [[China]]'s 80,000. (00:05:07) [[Richard Burr]], a [[Senator]] from [[North Carolina]] and member of the [[Senate Intelligence Committee]], sold his [[stock]] holdings in late February while publicly stating the [[United States]] had the coronavirus under control. (00:06:57) [[Tulsi Gabbard]] dropped out of the [[Democratic presidential primary]] and endorsed [[Joe Biden]]. (00:07:37) [[Tulsi Gabbard]]'s presidential campaign was primarily focused on opposing foreign regime change wars. (00:08:38) [[Joe Biden]] voted for the [[Iraq War]] in 2003 and, as [[Vice President]] under [[Barack Obama]], supported the [[NATO intervention in Libya]] that deposed [[Muammar Gaddafi]] and backed funding for the [[Syria|Syrian]] opposition against [[Bashar al-Assad]]. (00:11:43) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] stated his opposition to regime change in [[Syria]], the wars in [[Iraq]] and [[Afghanistan]], war with [[Iran]], and expressed skepticism of alliances with [[Saudi Arabia]], [[Qatar]], and [[Israel]]. (00:12:27) [[Tulsi Gabbard]] was a member of the [[Council on Foreign Relations]]. (00:16:50) [[Tulsi Gabbard]] was a frequent guest on [[Tucker Carlson]]'s show. (00:20:09) Donald Trump opposed unnecessary regime change wars. (00:20:57) Donald Trump worked to reduce illegal immigration, build a wall, restrict asylum seekers, and reduce levels of legal immigration. (00:21:32) Donald Trump opposed [[China]] and free trade, worked to bring troops home from [[Syria]], and negotiated an end to the war in [[Afghanistan]]. (00:22:19) Donald Trump authorized three rounds of missile strikes on [[Syria]] in 2017 and 2018. (00:22:52) The Donald Trump administration abandoned the [[Barack Obama]] administration's goal of regime change in [[Syria]] and announced a near-total withdrawal of [[U.S. Department of Defense|U.S. forces]] from [[Syria]] in August 2019. (00:27:26) The number of confirmed coronavirus cases in the [[United States]] increased from approximately 9,000 to nearly 14,000 in 24 hours. (00:28:04) Global confirmed coronavirus cases reached 246,328, with [[China]] reporting 80,928, [[Italian Republic|Italy]] at 41,035, [[Iran]] at 18,407, [[Spain]] at 18,077, [[Germany]] at 15,320, the [[United States]] at nearly 14,000, [[France]] at 10,995, and [[South Korea]] at 8,565. (00:30:53) The [[United States]] conducted 22,000 coronavirus tests in a single day, a significant increase from an estimated maximum capacity of 15,000-20,000 tests per day the previous week. (00:32:23) Drive-through coronavirus testing centers were established in [[Washington state]] and [[New York]]. (00:32:50) [[South Korea]] conducted an average of 15,000 coronavirus tests per day, which helped control its outbreak. (00:39:09) The [[The New York Times|New York Times]] reported that [[Senate]] [[Republican Party|Republicans]] unveiled an economic rescue plan providing hundreds of billions in loans to corporations and small businesses, corporate tax cuts, and $1,200 checks for taxpayers earning up to $75,000, with amounts tapering to zero for those earning over $99,000. (00:40:45) [[Mitch McConnell]], the [[Senate Majority Leader]], introduced the economic relief proposal after private talks with [[Republican Party|Republicans]] in the [[White House]]. (00:41:49) The [[Republican Party|Republican]] relief proposal included an additional $500 per child for eligible individuals and couples. (00:45:07) The [[Republican Party|Republican]] relief plan proposed moving the income tax filing deadline from April 15 to July 15. (00:45:56) The [[Republican Party|Republican]] plan authorized over $200 billion in support for hard-hit industries, including $50 billion for commercial airlines and $8 billion for air cargo carriers. (00:55:09) [[ProPublica]] reported that [[Richard Burr]], as chair of the [[Senate Intelligence Committee]], sold between $600,000 and $1.72 million in stocks in 33 transactions a week before the market declined by 30%, after receiving daily coronavirus briefings. (00:56:44) [[National Public Radio|NPR]] obtained a recording from February 27 in which [[Richard Burr]] gave a dire preview of the coronavirus's economic impact to a VIP group at the [[Capitol Hill Club]], whose members paid up to $10,000 for access. (00:57:55) In a February 7 op-ed, [[Richard Burr]] assured the public the [[United States]] was better prepared than ever for public health threats like the coronavirus. (00:58:18) [[Richard Burr]] sold up to $150,000 in shares of [[Wyndham Hotels & Resorts]] and up to $100,000 in shares of [[Extended Stay America]], companies whose values later plummeted.

### AF 2019-07-02 0419
(00:32:08) The [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] ruled that the Donald Trump administration could not add a citizenship question to the 2020 census, with Chief Justice [[John Roberts]] stating the administration's reasoning was insufficient but leaving open the possibility for a revised justification.

### AF 2019-07-02 0419
(00:35:56) The administration of Donald Trump chose not to challenge the [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]]'s ruling further and allowed the 2020 census forms to be printed without the citizenship question, effectively ending the legal dispute.

(00:52:51) The article accused [[Kaelin Robertson]] and [[George Lulyn John]] of stealing tens of thousands of dollars from [[Tommy Robinson]], defrauding [[Alex Jones]] with inflated expense claims, and endangering the lives of [[Gavin McInnes]] and [[Tommy Robinson]] by leaking their security information to [[the left]]-wing [[organization]] [[Hope Not Hate]]. (00:56:33) The article further alleged that [[Lauren Southern]] (real name [[Lauren Boebert|Lauren Simonson]]) was aware of and encouraged the activities of her producers, [[Kaelin Robertson]] and [[George Lulyn John]]. (01:01:21) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that the alleged actions of [[Kaelin Robertson]] and [[George Lulyn John]] demonstrated a pattern of problematic behavior when individuals not living conservative lifestyles, particularly women and homosexuals, are brought into conservative political movements. (01:06:41) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] characterized [[Milo Yiannopoulos]], [[Lauren Southern]], [[Kaelin Robertson]], and [[George Lulyn John]] as grifters motivated by money rather than genuine political conviction. (01:09:15) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] asserted that personal character is the most important factor for success in an insurgent nationalist political movement, citing the failures of [[Richard Spencer]] and [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] as examples of ideological and financial approaches undermined by character deficits. (01:25:39) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] stated that [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] received $15 million from the [[Mercer family]] after being fired from [[Breitbart News|Breitbart]] and disinvited from [[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]], but subsequently fell into debt and irrelevance due to personal character failings. (01:33:39) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] cited [[Mike Cernovich]] as an archetypal example of a grifter, noting his rapid shift from authoring "The MAGA Mindset" to disavowing Donald Trump, conservatism, and even [[Catholic Church|Catholicism]]. (04:44:49) Donald Trump threatened to impose a 5% tariff on all goods imported from [[Mexico]] on June 10th, 2019, unless [[Mexico]] changed its policies to reduce the flow of migrants across the [[United States]] border. (05:03:29) The Trump administration announced a deal with [[Mexico]] on Friday, June 7th, 2019, and suspended the threatened tariffs, claiming [[Mexico]] had agreed to deploy [[U.S. Department of Defense|National Guard]] troops to its southern border and to implement measures related to asylum seekers. (05:40:93) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] claimed that the details of the [[United States|U.S]].-[[Mexico]] deal were disputed, with the [[Mexican government]] stating there was no secret written agreement and only a commitment to review migrant flows in the future, while the Trump administration insisted a deal had been signed. (05:57:79) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that Donald Trump's retreat from the tariff threat followed a pattern of making threats on issues like immigration and then backing down, which he characterized as a strategy to convince voters of his seriousness ahead of the 2020 election rather than a genuine attempt to solve the problem. (07:03:29) The [[Vatican]] issued a document during Pride Month affirming traditional gender roles, which [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] praised as evidence that the [[Catholic Church]] is fighting globalism. (07:49:01) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] released a premium show analyzing the newly announced [[National Conservatism|National Conservatism Conference]], organized by [[Youram Hazony|Yoram Hazony]] and [[David Brog]], questioning its funding and associations. (08:50:59) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] discussed a [[The New York Times|New York Times]] article about former alt-right figure [[Caleb Cain]], criticizing it for contradictions, such as comparing the engagement of [[ContraPoints]] to [[Stefan Molyneux]]. (09:13:41) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] noted that [[Caleb Cain]]'s story of being radicalized and then de-radicalized by online content mirrored the alleged final actions of [[Kaelin Robertson]] and [[George Lulyn John]], [[who]] reportedly reached out to journalists claiming to have been de-radicalized after their activities were challenged. (13:36:39) [[Lauren Southern]] began her career as a content creator on the Canadian outlet [[Rebel Media]], alongside figures like [[Gavin McInnes]] and [[Faith Goldy]]. (14:34:25) [[Lauren Southern]] hired two homosexual producers, [[Kaelin Robertson]] and [[George Lulyn John]], [[who]] had previously worked for [[Rebel Media]] and [[Alex Jones|InfoWars]], to film her documentaries. (25:13:89) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] noted that [[George Lulyn John]] was reportedly a former transgender female [[who]] later identified as a gay man. (42:04:65) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] recalled that [[Lauren Southern]] had contacted him two years prior during the "Thought Wars" debate about the role of women in the right-wing movement, where he argued they were a net negative. (43:04:71) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] referenced the allegation that [[Kaelin Robertson]], brought into the movement by [[Lauren Southern]], leaked [[Gavin McInnes]]'s itinerary information to [[Antifa]], endangering his life. (46:01:61) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] stated that the proposed [[United States|U.S]].-[[Mexico]] deal included a "third safe [[country]]" agreement, which would allow the [[United States]] to reject asylum claims from migrants [[who]] passed through [[Mexico]] without first seeking asylum there, a provision he considered a potential game-changer. (48:54:31) Donald Trump tweeted that the [[United States|U.S]]. had "fully signed and documented" an immigration deal with [[Mexico]], but that it required a vote by [[Mexico]]'s legislative body and that tariffs would be reinstated if approval was not forthcoming. (49:40:43) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] compared the [[United States|U.S]].-[[Mexico]] immigration deal to other unfulfilled "agreements in principle" by the Trump administration, including denuclearization with [[North Korea]], a trade deal with [[China]], and the withdrawal of troops from [[Syria]]. (50:55:61) [[Marcelo Ebrard]], [[Mexico]]'s foreign minister, stated at a press conference that there was no secret [[United States|U.S]].-[[Mexico]] agreement, only an understanding to evaluate the flow of migrants in the coming months and consider further actions if numbers did not fall. (52:57:33) According to reports cited by [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]], part of the [[United States|U.S]].-[[Mexico]] deal involved [[Mexico]] deploying its [[U.S. Department of Defense|National Guard]] to its southern border with [[Guatemala]] and expanding the Migrant Protection Protocols (Remain in Mexico) policy. (53:44:19) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] clarified that the Migrant Protection Protocols, which allow asylum seekers to wait in [[Mexico]] while their claims are processed, were not the same as the more consequential "third safe [[country]]" agreement he had hoped for. (56:59:95) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] suggested that [[Mitch McConnell]] and congressional [[Republican Party|Republicans]] might have thwarted Donald Trump's tariff threat, but argued Trump should have let them override it to expose their opposition to his immigration agenda. (01:00:00.23) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that if Donald Trump were serious about immigration, he would have imposed the tariffs on [[Mexico]], dared [[Mitch McConnell]] to override them, and allowed [[Republican Party|Republican]] voters to revolt against the party for blocking the measure.

Fuentes]] characterized as a major disappointment and a strategic failure. (00:59:82) The address, which [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] analyzed live, was delayed from its original January 29th date after Donald Trump conceded to [[Nancy Pelosi]]'s demand to hold it in the [[House of Representatives]]. (01:06:34) Donald Trump's speech spent only approximately three minutes on immigration, a subject [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] identified as the president's most urgent priority, instead focusing on topics like the [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]], AIDS, and criminal justice reform. (02:13:86) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that Donald Trump's most independent presidential power is the "bully pulpit," his control over his own rhetoric and messaging to the [[United States|American]] people. (03:06:34) Donald Trump had previously ended the longest government shutdown in history without securing funding for a border wall, promising either a future deal or a [[state]] of emergency declaration by February 15th. (06:51:90) A [[White House]] official stated on [[Tucker Carlson]]'s program that an executive order to start building the border wall had been prepared for Donald Trump's desk immediately after his inauguration but was never signed. (07:18:96) Donald Trump's [[State of Illinois|State]] of the Union address included hawkish rhetoric on [[Venezuela]] and a "boomerish obsession with socialism," according to [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]]. (08:37:50) Donald Trump opened his speech by invoking the 75th anniversary of D-Day, with three D-Day veterans as special guests, and the 50th anniversary of the moon landing, with [[Buzz Aldrin]] as a special guest, to frame a theme of [[United States|American]] unity and greatness. (09:45:58) Donald Trump referred to the Allied liberation of [[Europe]] during D-Day as the "great crusade," a term originally used by [[Dwight Eisenhower]]. (11:45:60) [[Patrick Buchanan|Pat Buchanan]] wrote a column discussing the [[Ralph Northam]] blackface scandal in [[State of Virginia|Virginia]], connecting it to larger questions about [[United States|American]] historical memory. (13:38:22) Donald Trump devoted a segment of his speech to economic achievements, citing 5.3 million new jobs, 600,000 new manufacturing jobs, rising blue-collar wages, and record-low unemployment rates for [[black]], [[Hispanic]], Asian, and disabled [[United States|Americans]]. (15:00:24) Donald Trump campaigned on record-low minority unemployment rates during the 2018 midterms, but [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] noted that [[black]] and [[Hispanic]] voter support for him did not increase in response. (18:07:46) Donald Trump discussed criminal justice reform, highlighting racial disparities in sentencing, and featured Alice [[Boris Johnson|Johnson]], a nonviolent drug offender whose sentence he commuted, and [[Matthew]] Charles, the first prisoner released under the First Step Act, as special guests. (20:32:72) Donald Trump announced he was ordering an additional 3,750 troops to the [[United States|U.S]].-Mexico border during his speech. (20:53:10) The governor of [[State of New Mexico|New Mexico]] announced he was pulling [[U.S. Department of Defense|National Guard]] troops from the border on the same day as Donald Trump's [[State of Illinois|State]] of the Union address. (21:31:04) Donald Trump argued that "tolerance for illegal immigration is not compassionate" and listed negative impacts including reduced job opportunities, lower wages, overburdened schools, and increased crime. (21:55:88) Donald Trump's special guests on immigration included Deborah Bissell, whose family members were killed by illegal immigrants during a burglary, and Elvin Hernandez, an immigrant [[who]] became a [[U.S. Department of Homeland Security|DHS]] agent. (22:50:34) Donald Trump featured two [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] survivors as special guests during a segment of his speech addressing anti-Semitism and [[United States|U.S]]. support for [[Israel]]. (31:30:10) Donald Trump called on [[Congress]] to pass legislation to end late-term abortion, referencing [[Ralph Northam]]'s comments regarding executing a baby. (31:44:48) Donald Trump announced the [[United States|U.S]]. withdrawal from the Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces (INF) Treaty with [[Russia]] during his address. (32:11:98) Donald Trump announced his second summit with [[North Korea|North Korean]] leader [[Kim Jong Un|Kim Jong-un]] would be held on February 27th in [[Vietnam]]. Fuentes]] interpreted as a rebuke to [[Bernie Sanders]], [[Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez]], and [[Elizabeth Warren]]. ### AF 2019-01-24 0317

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:02:57) [[Corey Stewart]] previously ran in a 2017 special election for the [[U.S. Senate]] in [[State of Virginia|Virginia]], losing to [[Ed Gillespie]], and led Trump's 2016 campaign in the [[state]].

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:19:01) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] presented an analysis of President Trump's first 500 days in office, focusing on the economy, trade, immigration, and foreign policy.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:26:47) During the 2016 election, [[Barack Obama]] publicly doubted Trump's ability to bring back manufacturing jobs to the [[United States]].

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:27:34) The [[Tax Cuts and Jobs Act]] of 2017, passed under Donald Trump, reduced taxes for 90-95% of [[United States|Americans]] and cut the corporate tax rate from 35% to 21%.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:28:54) During Trump's presidency, 3 million jobs were created, including 304,000 manufacturing jobs and 337,000 construction jobs, and unemployment fell to 3.8%.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:29:06) The Trump administration cut 22 existing regulations for every new one introduced, representing the largest deregulatory effort in history.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:29:38) The [[United States]] GDP grew over 3% in the first three quarters of Trump's presidency, with the Atlanta [[Federal Reserve|Fed]] projecting 4.8% growth for a subsequent quarter.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:33:43) Trump's administration significantly degraded [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]], reducing its fighting force from approximately 100,000 to less than 1,000 and stripping it of major territorial holdings.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:35:46) Trump's foreign policy approach involved dual containment of so-called "rogue states" like [[Iran]], [[Syria]], [[Venezuela]], [[Cuba]], and [[North Korea]], utilizing maximum pressure and public deal-making rather than regime change wars.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:36:21) The Trump administration conducted two missile strikes against [[Syria]]: 59 Tomahawk missiles at a base in Homs in April 2017, and a coalition strike with [[France]] and the [[United Kingdom]] involving over 100 missiles on Damascus in April 2018.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:36:45) Trump withdrew the [[United States]] from the [[Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action]] ([[Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action|Iran nuclear deal]]) and re-imposed sanctions on [[Iran]].

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:40:01) Trump's "maximum pressure" campaign against [[North Korea]] led to a planned summit, the release of [[United States|American]] hostages, and the dismantling of a nuclear test site.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:41:06) The [[United States]] maintained a force of approximately 2,000 combat personnel and 5,000 contractors in [[Syria]] under Trump, [[who]] expressed a desire to withdraw them.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:42:03) The [[United States]] under Trump nearly completely withdrew from [[Iraq]] and maintained a troop presence in [[Afghanistan]] with the stated goal of finishing the mission before leaving.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:42:42) Trump pursued an assertive but cooperative relationship with [[China]], engaging in a trade war while cooperating on issues like [[North Korea]].

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:43:40) Trump's administration sought a reconciliation with [[Russia]] but faced obstacles from decades of mistrust and institutional hostility.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:44:53) Trump withdrew the [[United States]] from the [[Trans-Pacific Partnership]] and renegotiated a trade deal with [[South Korea]].

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:45:09) The Trump administration renegotiated the [[North American Free Trade Agreement]] and initiated a trade war involving tariffs on the [[Europe|European Union]], [[China]], [[Dominion of Canada|Canada]], and [[Mexico]].

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:48:58) On immigration, Trump rescinded the [[DACA]] program, though its implementation was delayed by court challenges, and increased [[U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement|ICE]] arrests of illegal immigrants by 42% in fiscal year 2017.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:50:51) Arrests of [[MS-13]] gang members increased by 83% under Trump's immigration enforcement policies.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:51:31) Trump repealed an [[Obama]]-era regulation that limited [[U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement|ICE]] enforcement to immigrants with violent crime convictions, expanding it to include those abusing welfare or with any criminal record.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:51:51) Trump deployed the [[U.S. Department of Defense|National Guard]] to the [[United States|U.S]].-Mexico border.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:51:51) Trump issued an executive order imposing a travel ban on several [[Islam|Muslim]]-majority countries, including [[Somalia]], [[Libya]], [[Iraq]], and [[Afghanistan]], which took effect after a vetting period.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:52:02) Trump drastically reduced the number of [[Syria|Syrian]] refugees admitted to the [[United States]] to approximately 100 per year, down from tens of thousands in prior years.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178
(00:53:52) Donald Trump stated he would never sign another spending bill like the 2018 omnibus after it failed to provide funding for a border wall.

### AF 2018-06-05 0178

(00:19:19) [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Bibi]] had for months been delivering English-language addresses from his desk aimed at a Western audience, calling for the liberation of the [[Iran]]ian people from their regime. (00:27:33) [[Richard Perle]] and [[Douglas Feith]], authors of the Clean Break Memo, later served in the [[United States]] [[U.S. Department of Defense|Defense Department]] under the [[George W. Bush]] administration and helped establish the [[Office of Special Plans]]. (00:28:28) The [[Office of Special Plans]], according to *The Guardian*, served as an unfiltered conduit for intelligence from the office of [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Israeli Prime Minister]] [[Ariel Sharon]], bypassing [[Israel Defense Forces|Mossad]] to provide the [[George W. Bush|Bush administration]] with alarmist reports on [[Saddam Hussein]]'s [[Iraq]]. Khan]]. (00:37:19) Donald Trump faces a May 12 deadline to decide whether to reimpose sanctions on [[Iran]], a move urged by [[Israel]] but opposed by [[Europe|European]] leaders like [[Emmanuel Macron]] and [[Angela Merkel]] [[who]] recently visited to preserve the nuclear deal. (00:40:08) A full-scale invasion and occupation of [[Iran]] to dismantle its nuclear program would require an estimated 250,000 [[United States]] soldiers and result in a longer, bloodier conflict than the [[Iraq War]] due to [[Iran]]'s larger population, robust military, and mountainous terrain. (00:42:25) [[Iran]]'s political and economic goals of reintegration into the global economy make the pursuit of a nuclear weapon counterproductive, unlike [[North Korea]]'s ideology of *Juche*, which prioritizes national self-sufficiency. (00:09:28) The budget proposal from Donald Trump allocated $18 billion for a border wall, funding for 2,000 [[U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement|ICE]] agents, 750 [[Customs and Border Protection|Border Patrol]] agents, and 75 immigration judges.

(00:09:35) Donald Trump's budget included $17 billion to combat the opioid epidemic and a $1.5 trillion infrastructure package, with only $500 billion funded federally.

(00:10:04) The proposed military spending increase under Donald Trump included modernization and expansion of the nuclear arsenal, contingent on other nations halting their own expansions.

(00:10:23) To finance new spending, Donald Trump's budget proposed $250 billion in cuts to [[Health and Human Services|Medicare]] through administrative reductions, not benefit changes, and imposed work requirements on the SNAP program.

(00:10:54) Donald Trump's budget proposed the repeal of the Affordable Care Act, the implementation of paid family leave programs by states, and cuts to funding for the arts, [[National Aeronautics and Space Administration|NASA]], and scientific research.

(00:11:11) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] argued that Donald Trump's proposed budget was unlikely to pass [[Congress]] due to opposition to the border wall and massive defense spending increases, requiring 60 votes in the [[United States Senate]].

### AF 2017-09-01 0007
(00:02:52) The [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] program, created under [[Barack Obama]], provides temporary legal status to certain undocumented immigrants [[who]] entered the [[United States]] as children. (00:03:04) Rumors circulated that Donald Trump was considering canceling the [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] program, with a final decision from [[the White House]] expected the following Tuesday. (00:03:26) [[Paul Ryan]] publicly urged Donald Trump to allow [[Congress]] to address and potentially preserve the [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] program through legislation. (00:04:20) The [[Republican Party]] had failed to repeal the [[Obamacare]] health law despite controlling both houses of [[Congress]] and campaigning on its repeal for years. (00:04:54) Donald Trump was elected on a platform that included ending [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] and removing undocumented immigrants from the [[United States]]. (00:05:19) In April, Donald Trump had stated he would keep [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]], causing concern among his immigration-restrictionist supporters. (00:05:42) Donald Trump was [[known]] to test public reaction to policy ideas through back-channel rumors before making a final decision. (00:06:27) Donald Trump, as [[White House|President]] of the [[United States]], has a primary duty to serve the interests of [[United States|American]] citizens over non-citizens. (00:08:46) [[Paul Ryan]] argued that [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] recipients were living in "limbo" and faced difficult circumstances. (00:11:12) In the 1980s, [[Ronald Reagan]] granted amnesty to approximately three million undocumented immigrants in exchange for promised border security measures that were never fully implemented. (00:25:38) An 88-year-old [[Germany|German]] woman named Ursula Haverbeck was sentenced to two years in prison by a court in [[Hamburg]] for [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] denial, a crime in [[Germany]]. (00:26:40) [[Ben Shapiro]], [[Dave Rubin]], and [[Steven Crowder]], prominent figures [[who]] advocate for free speech, were criticized for not speaking out against Ursula Haverbeck's imprisonment. (00:28:30) [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] denial is a criminal offense in 13 countries, including [[Germany]]. (00:31:19) [[Ben Shapiro]] built a multi-million dollar media career primarily on advocating for free speech and open discourse. (00:31:35) [[Ben Shapiro]]'s career was allegedly advanced through nepotistic connections, including his father's assistance in securing jobs and a book deal. (00:32:02) [[Ben Shapiro]]'s public platform frequently focuses on themes of free speech, civil discourse, and the open exchange of ideas. (00:35:50) [[Amazon Inc.|Amazon]]'s acquisition of [[Whole Foods]] was quickly approved by the [[U.S. Antitrust Commission]], which had several vacant seats at the [[time]].
